I (18M) am a high school senior who graduates in May. I'm stressed out with school and don't like the idea of college, but want to be happy and not struggle to survive. What do I do? (desc) | Hi all,
I'm 18M, from North Carolina.

I'm currently a senior in high school. I've generally gotten good grades. I'm currently in IB (International Baccalaureate) classes, which I think was personally the worst decision ever. The workload is tedious. I don't procrastinate but it still absolutely kills me.

I hear this is what college is like in the third and fourth year, and I don't want to deal with the mental health issues this has brought me. I dropped out of all the IB classes but two and that's still causing me extreme stress.

I'm planning on going to Wake Tech to get an Associate's, but after that, I'm not sure about college.

I want to get into Video Game Art, or at least I think I do. That is, environment design (how levels look, where things are placed, environment art, etc..).

I've heard that it's more about a portfolio and less about college for these types of jobs. Is that true?

My biggest worry is that either:
A.) I go to college, hate it, do badly, and end up with a useless major, crippling debt, and be so stressed and just completely waste 4 years of my life. I've gotten to the point where I absolutely despise school.

B.) Don't go to college, miss out on an opportunity and then struggle for the rest of my life trying to get anywhere.

I also worry if I really want to do environment art.
I find I get burnt out easily or not motivated to learn new things or work often on projects. But I do like doing it. Motivation has always been a big issue for me.

Although I've found more recently I think I like music more, although that is such a poor job decision I've heard, as you'll essentially always be poor unless you have some breakout success.

I don't want to be stuck doing some miserable job like retail forever. I don't know where to go from here. 

I get so depressed easily because I'm currently unemployed and feel awful like I'm just doing nothing, but these kinds of jobs suck. And I definitely don't want to go into anything with the military.

My mom on the other hand has said that most places won't just accept an Associate's, that I need a Bachelor's to be successful and stand out in the competition.

But I have friends who are much younger in college who say that's not the case. I'm torn. I trust my parents, but they haven't been in college for a very long time. My dad never went to college until he took some courses a couple years ago, but he's always been project manager pretty much. He's Head of IT at a company now.

I just want to be able to have a stress-free or minimal job, doing something I don't feel miserable doing, and get paid a decent amount to live comfortably and survive, while also having enough to maybe get something for myself for a little while  

Can anyone help me?

I was told that "My application would be considered when they were hiring" even though there was a "desperately hiring" sign outside. | Am I stupid? I filled out an application, gave it to them, then got brushed off after I spent 20 minutes filling something out. It's clear they're hiring, they have a "Desperately hiring" sign outside. The place I was applying to was a mom-and-pop pizza place who have so little employees they shut down their inside dining because there's no hosts.

A polite (won’t get me fired) way to remind my team I’m not in their time zone? | I’m the only person on the team not in the same time zone. I’m new and I think they are not used to it, or maybe already forgot. I don’t bother them at 4am their time when I’m getting logged in for the day. But I’m getting really tired of attending late meetings for them that are basically at dinner time for me. One time a late meeting was starting to run over and I just politely said that we should be mindful of the time. Another time I said “oh that’s actually pretty late for me being in my time zone”. It’s not seeming to work. I asked my boss if I should shift my hours and she said no. Kind of at a loss. On days that I know I have late meetings, I just start my day a little later. But I feel like I’m having to remind them often. Any ideas?

Isn't luck involved more than people let on? | I get that hard and smart work help you to achieve success, but isn't luck a big part of it? It just so happens you can increase your luck through perseverance? Like you have to be at the right place at the right time with the right people? Is it wrong for me to assume this is a missing puzzle piece most people leave out of their success stories?

Just as an example, if you apply and begin working for a certain company, what if you land in a culture that fits you perfectly and you climb up the rungs in 3 years whereas what if you were in one that didn't fit you and the people there just didn't mesh with you as a person and you work hard and trudge away for the same time for less return? Only for you to have to leave the job and hope you find a company that will hire you that will work well with you.

In this example, it's luck that you only had to try once to get into a culture that fit you, but the second one you have to try two or more times over many years to find if a workplace would be right for you to succeed, assuming you ever find that workplace or connection.

Merely an example above. Please know I'm also asking about how it's luck that people like you enough as a person to give you connections and a job and other such things.
I don't understand why some people who complain a lot at work don't change jobs?

Alert company ASAP of resignation immediately or wait until after Thanksgiving Holiday? | Hello!

I'm feeling pretty bad about leaving my current job in a serious lurch by leaving. We are a team of four (part of a larger organization), one of our team quit Nov 1, and the person they had lined up to fill the spot declined after weeks of interviewing, so we've been working one down since with no end in sight. 

I've received another formal offer that I just absolutely can't turn down - it's about 30k more a year, 1.5 hours taken off my daily commute, etc., so I'm leaving as well. Due to the holidays I am able to give them about 7 weeks notice.

I know that they will want to know ASAP - just wondering if it would be more above board/professional to do it before the Thanksgiving Holiday (we have Friday off as well) - or wait til Monday.

Any and all advice is appreciated!

Gave my two weeks because WFH was ending. Today my employer announced those who want to keep WFH can do so indefinitely. Should I stay or commit to leaving? | I've posted a few times already here and on the jobs subreddit, so apologies if people are getting tired of hearing from me, lol.

Basically, my employer announced a few months ago that we were returning to the office by the end of the year. After waiting to see if they would reconsider after quite a few people resigned, I determined that leadership wasn't going to change their minds about this, so I started applied for fully-remote jobs. I landed one last week and gave my two-weeks to my boss. This was not easy because I genuinely enjoy working here. The return-to-office policy is the only reason I went through with this.

The new job pays more than double my salary and is in the same field, but I've been nervous about it. It's corporate, and I have only had bad experiences in the corporate field. The new role is geared more toward my weaknesses (planning, strategy, coordination between departments) than my strengths (coding, design, technical work). Apparently it's a layoff-prone company. I've been feeling that I was too hasty in accepting the first offer made to me in this job search. But, again: fully remote and pays more than double what I'm making now.

Today my employer announced that those who want to return to office can, those who want to work hybrid can, and those who want to be fully remote can. The sole reason for my departure no longer applies. Had I known this, I would not have taken the job offer. This feels like an indirect counter-offer, so I'm approaching with caution.

**Should I forge ahead with the new job or stay now that my sole reason for leaving no longer applies?**

I know it's not as easy as me going "well, nevermind, then, I'm staying!" If I stayed, my boss -- who I get along with very well and was very supportive of my decision -- would see me as a flight risk. They would know I've been looking to jump ship. I would look prone to making panic decisions (which I am, but I try to post a hundred threads asking for help on Reddit first). I would be settling for a lower salary.

But if I leave, I could end up in a job that isn't right for me. I'm already getting bad vibes from it. If it doesn't work out, my current job might not take me back.

What would you do in this situation?

**UPDATE:** Employer clarified that this is *temporary* and they will still require to come back to the office full time at an indeterminate date. Think my mind is made up on leaving at this point.
Applied at midnight, rejected at 2:00AM

AMA Announcement! Mike Kabongo - Technical Recruiter @ Google | Howdy, greetings, salutations, felicitations and all that! I am Mike Kabongo: cook turned security guard turned salesman, turned recruiter. I'm currently on a contract at Google; the last company I worked for had an even larger headcount.

My musical tastes are pretty broad, and my reading list spans more areas.

My LinkedIn and Twitter profile links are below, and I'm here to do an AMA over Reddit Talk for r/jobs. Because I've switched careers more than once, worked as a hiring manager, interview panel member, and as a (mostly) technical recruiter, I offer a different perspective than most folks.

So get your questions ready, bring a friend and a frenemy too, so you can Ask Me Anything! **3PM ET on Thursday 4/21 is the time.** [https://www.linkedin.com/in/mike-kabongo/](https://www.linkedin.com/in/mike-kabongo/) [https://twitter.com/Mike\_Drop](https://twitter.com/Mike_Drop)\_

​

# Edit:

Thank you to the Mods for picking me, thank you to everyone who came and asked questions. I got asked for some additional advice here, and I can share it as I have time. I do regularly repost older LinkedIn stuff to my Twitter account and that's probably a less painful way to find the job search, and resume stuff versus doom-scrolling my LinkedIn post history.

I got a bachelor’s degree in psychology, worked in marketing, as a personal trainer, taught abroad for 2 years and now work as an engineer through developing software skills. Everyday my mind tears me apart telling me I should be a doctor and I should be putting my energy into helping others. I was always passionate about anatomy, neuroscience, psychology, nutrition and language. 

I’m at a point to feel like I will look back on life and hate that I didn’t do something more meaningful. (For myself) I feel no meaning in my current work.
I have 80k saved and want to find a different pathway forward. I’d be willing to get my education anywhere in the world. I feel so lost and like such a loser. My gfs dad is a doctor who has helped so many people and I am sitting in an office all day. 

Since I have a bs, I would basically need like 5 classes (about 1. 5 years more undergrad) and then a ton of MCAT studying to get in. I’d like to avoid debt which is why I would ideally do it in another country.

I’m sorry if this sub isn’t meant for this kind of post. I just don’t know where to start or where/who to reach out to.

How to deal with a firm that gives me a guilt trip trying to leave the firm? | Current employer had been promising me a promotion for the past 3 years and were just about to promote me in the next 3 months, when I got a lateral offer from outside with a stronger reputation and higher comp (20%).

I tried to resign stating the lack of promotion as a reason, but the business head messaged and told me I am foolish to leave now because I am sooo close to getting the promotion I wanted all along. I told him I want to resign unless he makes the promotion happen now, which he refused.

Afterwards, he went around all the managers in the firm saying I am a disloyal employee who can't reciprocate trust. Now everyone in the firm thinks I am a person who tried to use a "dirty trick" to get a promotion which backfired against me.

I feel so bad and I am afraid of the damage that's done on my reputation. I tried to tell my colleagues about my version of the story, but obviously the entire firm has turned against me. What do I do?

Resign or Accept PIP | Today my boss's boss and HR suddenly called me (we're a fully remote work force) to tell me "they don't think I'm a right fit for the company" and to get back to him in a week on what option I wanna do:

1) Resign in 30 days
2) Be placed on a 90-day PIP which (and I quote): "will likely not end well for you"

So that had a bit of a threatening/foreboding tone. 2 1/2 months ago during my review I was told (and it was documented)  I was doing phenomenal and I'm a tremendous asset to the company. About a month and a half ago my boss was telling me I have the highest potential and capabilities of all his team.

So...this is new, but it's also about a month and a half after I filed an HR complaint

I really haven't been given objective feedback on where I'm falling short. Still trying to figure that one out. Interesting to see what the PIP would say, I've never had one before in my 18 years of being in the workforce. But I have a week to pick an option.

Thoughts?

Would you refer a coworker who wouldn’t refer you? | Recently left a job for another company in sane industry but different department. Pretty disappointed to find that almost all my old coworkers have ghosted me, even the ones I would hang out with outside of work.

Before I left, they talked about how it’s cool having a connection at another company but I don’t think I would want to refer anyone based on how I’ve been ghosted. Also I realized how toxic most of my old coworkers are after seeing how nice my new coworkers are, so now I feel it’s just a culture fit issue

So overall, would you guys still refer someone who would probably ignore you if you asked for a recommendation? Or would it be petty not to refer since I can’t deny that a lot of them did good work

Edit: couple people getting angry over this question. Just to be clear, I’m asking what others would do because I’m not sure what I would do personally. Not looking to justify one mindset or the other

Do you get stressed in your job a lot? Would you quit a $100k+ IT job for something stress-free and pays like $50k? | Right now I'm working in a position where I troubleshoot/design projects for clients, and I hate it. I really, really hate it. It's stressful. I feel like it's affecting my day-to-day interactions with people where I'm becoming an asshole.  
   
Every morning I wake up and dread the day, I turn on my computer and MS Teams flashes with a million messages from a million people, all of which need to be addressed. There's no "cruise control" with this job anyway, it's not like you learn one thing and bam, you just repeat it...it's anything but monotonous but I'm sick of constantly having to use my brain all the time where it's high alert. Everything is urgent, they're always on your ass to get numbers/projects done, it's just too much.   
  
I really don't know if this is a question I should be asking here or if it's something I need to sit down and contemplate for a long time, but I would love to hear your thoughts.   
  
    
   
Have you ever been in a similar situation? Was the pay cut worth it? I don't know, I just feel lost.

I started ignoring 3rd party recruiters and it's been great. | Just as the title says, for the last 2 years or so I've been ignoring and shutting down every 3rd party recruiter who hits me up on LinkedIn. When I was a bit more junior I used to entertain these types of recruiters with the mindset of "No stone unturned" or "No avenue unexplored" - each and every time it was a complete failure. It was always a contract job or a job with a company not large enough to have its own recruiters. Anytime I gave a salary requirement that I felt was competitive, I was always met with some ridiculous senior level director type position because the companies the represent pay their management what larger tech companies pay their regular developers.  


These people represent the bottom % of tech jobs. There is no golden ticket to be found. I think people can benefit greatly from the same realization.

Defeated. Any leads? | Like my title suggests I think I need a little help. I graduated at 40 in 2016. I’m from a very small town so for about five years I did some programming at small local places, a couple of small contracts then covid hit. I finally got a break in February with a contract government position as a full time java programmer with the explosion of remote work. 
I was saving up to relocate so that when/if the time comes I could find a job easier in a bigger area in case remote dies down. 

Well, in the start of July we had a “stop work” order on the contract. I waited a couple of weeks as no one had any idea if we were coming back or not. I was two weeks away from moving. I then started to look for work. In the process I had interviewed with a handful of companies and I finally found a dream job with a big company. The process was fairly lengthy and I was eating away at my savings between staying here at a quick rental I found and normal bills. I don’t qualify for unemployment here in PA because I was out of work for about six months before I got the government contract and I don’t have enough credit weeks in. 

Today I got a call that the company was retracting the offer. (I actually never saw the offer as they were working it up while my paperwork was being done). It appears that on my background check there was a discrepancy between how long I worked at a company a few years ago. It looks like I made a transposition error. I never got notice of this in writing, this came from my recruiter that set me up with the company as it was a full time permanent position. 

Anyway, I’m back to square one and I am desperate and defeated. I got about a month of savings left and this really put a hurting on me and my family. I have a bad back and severe asthma so that limits what I can do even if I wanted to just get anything. Being in a small town surrounded by trees doesn’t help either. 
So, if anyone has any leads for a junior to mid level java position, I would greatly appreciate it. 
Thanks everyone for reading, have a good evening.

We compiled a library of realistic engineering take-home tests and ranked them |  

[https://www.trytapioca.com/library-of-assessments](https://www.trytapioca.com/library-of-assessments)

Studies show that a work sample test is the best predictor of candidate performance on the job, which is why many software engineering teams use take-home tests as one step in their hiring process. But designing an effective test is difficult and time-consuming. For example, candidates are reluctant to complete tests that are too long or not engaging enough. But make them too short and teams won’t get the signal they need for a proper evaluation.

To encourage more thoughtful test design (and hopefully save future candidates from the worst offenders), my team compiled the *largest library of non-“whiteboard” take-home tests* that real engineering teams have used. You’ll find the challenges that Stripe and Microsoft gave to their full-stack candidates, front-end tests from Tailwind and Rivian, and back-end ones from Basecamp and Revolut. Whether you’re looking to evaluate an Android, DevOps, or Data Science candidate, a bootcamp grad, or senior engineer, we found a few options for each.

Having built 20+ tests ourselves, we also rated the design of each test. The criteria for a 5-star rating:

1. Tests for skills highly relevant to those required for the position

2. Includes a well-written description of the prompt and even motivation for using a take-home test

3. Sets clear expectations for candidates (e.g. time requirements, evaluation criteria, submission details)

4. Asks for a reasonable time commitment from candidates (<4 hours)

A few notes: - We found most of these test prompts in public GitHub repos, usually owned by the hiring team but occasionally in the candidate-owned submission. We sifted through hundreds of tests and filtered out those overly focused on algorithms (aka LeetCode), leaving us with 142 tests in the library.

\- The larger and more recognizable companies didn’t always have the best tests. Some of the most interesting prompts we found were from smaller teams (e.g. YC startups). This shouldn’t be surprising. Startups need to design candidate-friendly hiring experiences to compete for talent against more established players.

\- There were common themes among the tests we found. For example, front-end candidates were often given a Figma design + content feed to implement, while back-end candidates had to implement an API given a set of requirements. Data scientists were usually given a data set to clean, analyze, and submit a Jupyter notebook with their findings.

\- We’ll continue to update this library and add descriptions of each test so it’s easier to compare.

Have feedback, or another take-home test we should add? We’d love to hear from you!

New grad job misrepresented my role/responsibilities. A year later and I’m struggling to leave due to lack of relevant experience. How do I escape this? | Last year, I accepted a job at a very small company (mistake #1) in the defense industry. By small, I mean <10 employees and only 3 other engineers at the time, now only 1 other engineer. My undergrad was focused on embedded systems, mainly C development and FPGA development in Verilog. I was hired at this company to do FPGA work because of my background. I was told I would also be doing C++ development for embedded Linux platforms. 

Fast forward to today, nearly a year later. 80-90% of my job is software integration. Not even DevOps really, we legitimately receive an SDK from another vendor and use a diff tool to copy code changes into our client’s larger baseline software suite.  No streamlined/automated build process. I write software installation scripts. I install software on payloads. And I verify the software works. That’s the bulk of this job. I get tasked with integrating other vendor’s apps into the aforementioned SDK, except the vendor developed their software with an SDK version that is quite literally years older than the most recent. And it’s my job to integrate despite that the SDK has changed massively since then. 

Since November I’ve been applying and trying to leave. I think my resume is fine, as I consistently get phone/tech interviews for roles that are relevant to what I want to do and did in the past in undergrad (embedded development mainly). I’ve had probably 5-6 promising interviews. 

The problem is I cannot keep up with even technical questions in phone screens, let alone actual technical interviews. The breadth of questions I receive for these interviews, even for 1 year of experience, is just difficult for me to keep up with given the fact that I use absolutely none of it in my current role. I’m absolutely regressing as a software engineer and it’s only getting worse the longer I stay here. 

How do I escape this? It seems like a catch 22. For the roles I’m interested in, I need experience in development whereas my current role only gives me experience in integration. The more “experience” I have, the more I’m expected to know for those roles, except my knowledge only tends to regress. Any advice? I do my best to keep up to speed during my free time but it’s more difficult than I thought it would be.

Have you used a salary negotiation service? | Hello Redditors,

I'm currently taking a sabbatical from work but I'm planning to start looking for something new in March. I've seen services like those from levels.fyi for salary negotiation and I'm curious about whether doing so would be worthwhile for me.

* Have you used this type of service?
* If so, who provided the service?
* How much did it cost?
* Do you feel like it was worth the money you paid?

For context, I have over 20 years of development experience, with about 10 in technical lead positions. I'm going to be looking for either an IC position at a level above senior (lead, staff, principal) or a combined IC/management position that manages a team of engineers and also has technical leadership responsibilities. If you've used a service for a negotiation at a similar level please note that in your response, since that will be extra relevant.

Thank you for your input!

I GOT IT | I GOT MY FIRST OFFER FOR A SOFTWARE ENGINEERING INTERNSHIP POSITION FUCK YES! I’m a sophmore cs major and applied to at least 100 diff jobs, got 3 interviews and 1 offer. So thankful for it and I feel like the hardest part is finally over, but let me know if I’m wrong :/ I definitely feel like landing very the first internship is always the hardest part of a cs career.
Barbers of reddit do you like your job ? Please tell me why you chose barbering and was it worth it | Im Starting a barbering course at college .

LC solutions grinding | Let's be honest.

All West Coast SW companies say they want to see "how you arrive at a solution", but the dude interviewing you has memorized 100 LC problems, you've memorized 200 and you both sit there and pretend neither of you memorized them and go through a complex dance of acting like you thought of the answer on the fly.

You and the person who is interviewing you have a vested interest in maintaining the LC cycle.

It's just as bad with the system design questions, as those are also all over the internet. They are just easier to memorize.

At least golf balls in a 747 is an engineering estimation problem.
[deleted by user]

I can't believe I'm saying this... I finally got an offer!!! | Keep your head up if you're a pandemic college graduate! I've been job searching off and on for about 5 months, and applied to 220 roles solely on LinkedIn. I interviewed at 45 places, including phone screens. I had final interviews at 14 companies. I was trying to make the jump from customer service and sales to HR with no immediate industry connections.

The biggest game changer for me was having an informational interview with an HR higher-up a month ago. They encouraged me to slightly alter the wording in one of my job titles on my resume. My title had pigeonholed me into the sales industry despite my job description being full of transferable HR skills. After doing that, I started getting interviews left and right and knew it was only a matter of time.

There were a few points in my search where I felt like I would never get there (especially after final interviews), and applying to jobs felt like a useless waste of time. Don't give up hope. It's all a numbers game, and the right one is out there.

From overworked to lazy? | When I first started my CS career I always heard about how developers were overworked. I heard about things like unrealistic requirements and “death marches”. Sure the pay was good but you’d be expected to work long and hard hours.

Now it seems to be the opposite. All I hear about is how developers are lazy and do basically nothing all day (sleeping till nod) and cash giant pay checks.

When did this change? Is there any truth to it or is it just memes on the Internet? Is it going to be a problem for the industry if the narrative now is that developers are basically over paid for what they do?

How do you not let your career outcomes become your identity? | I didn't get the full-time return offer after a dream internship. I associated having the full-time title at that (or any other) big N as a license which proved to people I am actually capable in my field.
